content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies that are better going in blind

**Score:** 52
**Body:** Need suggestions for movies that are better going in WITHOUT reading the overview/description.

Been going down a rabbit hole recently where I’ll let movies auto play after I finish one. Allows me to watch movies without knowing ANYTHING about them, no idea what the plot is, makes seeing familiar/popular actors more exciting too. I find that reading the description has often spoiled a movie for me or left me anticipating a certain plot-point then being let down by its execution.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. All of them
2. Gone Girl
3. I just watched The Coffee Table, not knowing anything about it.  It is a good film.  Best to go in blind.
4. Oh my god please pretty please go watch From Dusk Till Dawn. This is the perfect one. Do it. Now.
5. Stranger than Fiction (2006)

The Prestige (2006)

The Sting (1973)
6. Dark City, The Thirteenth Floor, Barbarian are the first ones that come to mind for me.
7. I really liked Moon (2009) Watched it blind, wasn't disappointed.
8. Hard Candy.
9. Most of them IMO. 
10. Everything Everywhere All At Once

## 2. What movies really feel better on a big screen?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** My projector just arrived and after a bit of testing, it already feels very different from watching on a TV. Once the room is dark, the scale and immersion really stand out. 

This weekend I’m planning a proper movie night with my girlfriend, so I’m trying not to waste the first pick. 

I’m currently using an n1s 4k, and instead of demo material, I’m more curious about movies that genuinely benefit from a big screen. Stuff where the scale, atmosphere, or visuals actually add to the experience. Any genre is fine. Your suggestions are really appreciated.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Christopher Nolan movies. 

Any Imax Enhanced 4K discs.
2. Interstellar hits different on a big screen, especially those space scenes - the sound design alone will blow your mind. Blade Runner 2049 is another solid pick, the cinematography is absolutely stunning when you can see all those details
3. f1
4. Any epic. Lawrence Of Arabia, 2001, Dune, Ben Hur, Dr Zhivago, Ran, Interstellar, Lord Of The Rings, Titanic, Good Bad Ugly, Once Upon A Time In either America or the West, Andrei Rublev, probably John Ford's entire filmography, etc.
5. More for the sound than the (also great) visuals, but Das Boot is incredible, especially the four hour director’s cut. Best submarine movie ever made. Not joking.
6. A lot of fucking movies. A LOT! Since 2017-18 my local regal has played a ton of movies from the 50’s - 00’s. 

I have a pretty sweet home theater set up but there’s just something different seeing a movie in theaters, especially those that I wasn’t alive for the initial release.
7. Ran (1985)


Loving Vincent (2017)


The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014)


The Cook, the Thief, His Wife & Her Lover (1989)
8. Pretty much all of them, I would say. even a simple quiet drama without any fancy cinematography is enhanced by a good loud sound system and a huge screen in a dark room.

its a boomer choice but If I really wanted to test out a new home cinema setup i'd probably put on 2001 a space oddessey first, just because it is such an iconic audio-visual experience. Suspiria would be another good choice, anything that relies on the intensity of its soundtrack and its colours. Stuff with high contrast.
9. Skyfall

Life of Pi

Planet Earth series

The Fall (2006)
10. Interstellar!

## 3. What to watch with my daughter after Back to the Future 1,2 and 3

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Hi all

We recently watched Back to the Future 1,2 and 3 - we being me and my 10yo daughters. She loved them - as did I (again LOL) I'd love some suggestions for what else we can watch. We did Ferris Bueller, Princess Brida, BIG, and a bunch of Studio Ghibli - and I have thought about Raiders of the lost ark - but she's not keen.

Teen Wolf was another - which I haven't seen.

So my learned friends I'd love your suggestions.

thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [Adventures in Babysitting (1987)](https://reelgood.com/movie/adventures-in-babysitting-1987) - Elisabeth Shue (Jennifer from *Back to the Future II & III*) has a crazy night when she takes the kids she is babysitting with her, when she goes to rescue a friend at a bus station.
2. You doing an 80's theme? Labyrinth
3. The Goonies is perfect for this! Same adventure vibe as BTTF and totally age appropriate. Also maybe try The Karate Kid - classic 80s feel and she'll probably love the underdog story

  
If she's into the sci-fi aspect, Short Circuit is super fun and not too intense. E.T. might work too if she hasn't seen it already
4. Honey I Shrunk the Kids (1989) is excellent.

As an adult you can appreciate the parental characters as well as the production design while the kids can appreciate the action, adventure and the humour.

For bonus points, Honey I Blew Up the Kid (1992). I hadn’t seen it since shortly after it came out until a few months ago and it was surprisingly delightful. It’s basically a Godzilla movie for kids; really good and a lot of fun.
5. Ghostbusters

Beetoven

Mrs Doubtfire

Three Men and a Baby

The Great Outdoors

Crocodile Dundee
  - Love this list! Thanks
6. Who Framed Roger Rabbit for more Christopher Lloyd
7. - War Games (1983)
- Bill and Teds Excellent Adventure (1989)
- The Chronicles or Narnia
- Second Hand Lions
8. The Mummy 1-3
9. Labyrinth

Legend

The Dark Crystal

Return to Oz

The Neverending Story

## 4. Suggestions for horror!

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Looking to watch some horror movies I may have not seen before. Would you guys be able to give me 5 horror movies I should watch? Possibly your favorites of all time?

Thanks :)


EDIT: I am a horror fan so I’m looking for movies that’s not as popular as let’s say Halloween. Sorry I should’ve clarified.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Get Out

Hereditary 

The Evil Dead 1 & 2

The Shining

A Nightmare on Elm Street
2. Altered 

From Beyond

Dog Soldiers
3. The Boxer's Omen

The Reflecting Skin

The White Reindeer

The Texas Chainsaw Massacre 2

Poultrygeist: Night of the Chicken Dead
4. Living dead at Manchester morgue 

We are still here 

The sentinel 

Cobweb 

City of the living dead
5. There's plenty of classics that will be on every top 10 list and horror is super subjective and spans a lot of genres, some are so bad they're great, but here's some favorites as far as must plain good movies, mostly newer except a few. Check out /r/horror if you're interested in horror

Bring Her Back

Hellbender

Speak No Evil (the original version, not the American remake) 

Hereditary 

Barbarian

Drag Me to Hell

Antichrist

Together

The Rule of Jenny Penn

In a Violent Nature

American Psycho

The Menu

Pearl, X, and MaXXXine in that order

Longlegs

The Killing of a Sacred Deer
6. Evil Dead 2

Re-Animator

Behind the Mask: The Rise of Leslie Vernon

The Ninth Gate

Texas Chainsaw Massacre 2
7. House of 1000 Corpses 

Drag Me to Hell
8. High Tension would compliment most of these lists well
9. I loved Smile 2, mainly due to the incredible leading performance. Worth it for her 

Pearl

Candyman

Original Suspiria 

Nostalgic, guilty pleasure: Gothika, Children of the Corn, Hide and Seek
10. Rosemary’s Baby is a classic. A masterclass in horror. Highly recommend it. 


Then a random mishmash:


Talk to Me


The Autopsy of Jane Doe



Cabin in the Woods



As Above So Below

## 5. Comfort horror or comedies

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Hey! So I’m sick, and I’m looking for some films to watch while I’m miserable.

I’m open to mostly comedies and horror movies (nothing too heavy). During this, I’ve enjoyed together, the perks of being a wallflower, Zoolander, and pitch perfect 2, if that helps.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Final Girls and What we do in the Shadows are great horror comedies
2. Tremors and Tucker and Dale Vs Evil are two movies that fit comedy-horror as a vibe
  - Tucker and Dale is a national treasure!! Such a great movie!
3. Shaun of the Dead
  - It fulfills both the comedy and horror requests
4.  Tremors
5. Slither. It's both.
6. Army of Darkness
7. The tv series What We Do in the Shadows.
8. Return of the Living Dead. "Brains!"

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What are the current storytelling devices, tropes, and other tricks directors and producers currently use, which will seem obviously quaint or overly manipulative and dated in another generation?

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I remember speaking with a music producer we talked about how agonizing it is to watch a movie and hear the artificial suspense built up by the music trying to elicit certain feelings from the audience. It was so over the top and manipulative and any keen cinemaphile we'll see right through it and get turned off.

The more non-industry movie Watchers learn about movie making, The more obvious these tools will be. So directors and producers will by necessity have to become more creative in their storytelling. I'm curious to know what other devices you see are being used that will be so painfully obvious in the years to come?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The tricks, devices, and tropes you’re talking about are *part* of the story-telling method.

Think of the final fight between Prince Phillip and Maleficent in Sleeping Beauty. The music as it builds and crashes. The colours - purple green & black v blue white & red. The fight with Phillip on top of the cliff, and the dragon below him. When she climbs onto the cliff and towers over him, and he loses his shield.

They’re all very obvious “tricks”. Music. Colour theory. Good v evil. But they help tell the story.
2. > we'll see right through it and get turned off.

I want to question this. Why does noticing it turn you off?

Does it turn you off that I started this sentence with “does it”, foreshadowing that it would probably end with a question mark? Because, to me, that is just understanding language.

Similarly, noticing what a soundtrack is doing, knowing what emotions a sort of music is supposed to evoke, doesn’t bother me. I *want* the music to correctly match the scene’s intended tone.

A lot of “cinephiles” approach movies adversarially, and I truly don’t get it. I don’t need to outsmart the director or vise versa. I need to understand what they’re communicating.
  - To add…If something feels dissonant from the score, perhaps it’s because other elements of the movie are failing to match the timeless emotion that the music evokes.

OP might be overanalyzing at the expense of experiencing. Or maybe they’re simply referring to poor, overly dramatic scores. Without specific examples, it’s unclear. I might agree that a certain piece of music feels “hand hold-y”. Sometimes, audience manipulation is crude (canned laugh tracks) and does feel dated.
3. I just enjoy stories. Don’t overthink it.
  - Then why choose to click on a thread that's clearly for those who are interested in doing more than that?
  - Rofl. So, because you “just” enjoy stories, you’re commanding other people to stop thinking about whatever they’re thinking about? Just making sure, because the way you framed it sounds so casual
4. I have no idea why people are giving you a hard time about this. Seems a fair enough question.

I don't think 'manipulative' music is going anywhere, it's too useful and absolutely imbedded.

I think the 'girl boss' main character will age badly. 

The whole 'gender swap reboot' phase was terribly lazy and misjudged. Story should come before novelty.

The sassy best friend.

Saved in the nick of time.

I hope we move away from superhero movies, or at least learn to make these stories differently.

We need to stop treating every film as a microcosm of society.

On that subject, it'd be nice to see more villains who aren't white and straight. Villains are great roles, we should stop being over sensitive about it and let everyone have a go.

Let's see some darker films for kids, stop talking down to the audience. Family films are generally too soft these days

I also personally dislike how the dog almost always survives.

... Most tropes are useful. Generally, there aren't that many bad devices, just devices done badly.
  - > I also personally dislike how the dog almost always survives.


You monster!
    - :D
5. I see it more from the screenwriting side: Front loading films with a momentous first 10 pages / 10 minutes. 

A few things: marginalization-porn. Sean Baker's films are good enough, but his imitators aren't. Hyper Literal villains (Perfidia Beverly Hills!?) a hyper-literal moralizing (Dont Look Up, Promising Young Women,   
Never Rarely Sometimes Always, etc). The general restraint of modern films, the un-horniness of them.

## 2. My Review of Small, Slow but Steady (2022)

**Score:** 3
**Body:** "She has a big heart"

How many times can the wheel really be reinvented? Tires, Life Preservers, Hula Hoops, Donuts? What was I talking about?...

Anyway, this question seems to be at the heart of Small, Slow But Steady. Director Sho Miyake knows what you wanna see. He dances around Boxing Tropes with ease and deft like a boxer weaving in and out of punches. Not with malice but love. This IS a boxing movie, through and through, and the attention to detail that Miyake employs is more than evidence of that fact. 

Yukino Kishii plays "Keiko," a deaf Chinese Woman who takes up professional boxing as a sort of hobby. One of the main ways this movie eschews genre conventions, right off the bat, is making Keiko an accomplished competitor from the beginning. Initially, I was worried about this (being a big boxing movie fan myself) but as the film warms up so does its themes and this decision ends up freeing the movie in unexpected ways. The movie chooses instead to focus on other things: Why does Keiko fight, the gym she trains in, a lot of character work is given over to her mentor in the move. Director Sho knows you want the big anthemic Eye of the Tiger-montage rags-to-riches story but this movie is much closer to a slice of life drama than anything like that. Although there is still a flashback montage in this movie, just another way that Sho likes to play around with what the audience wants; giving it to them but only on his terms

What really stood out to me, is all the attention to small details. This really manifests in two ways for me: One is Deaf Culture in general. You really get a good sense of how Deaf people are treated in our society. Keiko carries around a writing utensil and a little board with her, she uses a lot of vibration and light cues, people often shout at her thinking if they speak louder she'll suddenly be able to hear them. Her deafness is used as a tool to show how ostracized Keiko feels throughout the movie

The other detail is the actual boxing: this movie really centers boxing as physical art. Keiko is small, often captured on camera in wide shots, far away. She rarely reaches the shoulder of the other characters in the movie. So she wins fights by being nimble, trained, quick. This is explicitly stated in the movie but also shown in the choreography and its so impressive to see.

It's not my favorite boxing movie ever, but I appreciate what it brings to the (scorer's) table. It may not reinvent the wheel completely but its a damn fine wheel

Reviewed on Letterboxd: https://boxd.it/ctHmNn

Thank you!

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. How are so many people misunderstanding Marty Supreme

**Score:** 95
**Body:** 
Marty is a complete pos, narcissist, who will do absolutely anything to achieve his “dream.” You are not supposed to like him, the guy nearly gets his unborn child murdered all in pursuit of winning a game of ping pong, it’s literally the most absurd personification of hustle culture possible. That’s the whole point in the films ending, it’s not even complex, did people just close their eyes through the ending scenes. He does all that wins the game and it doesn’t fill the hole it meant nothing. The guy literally breaks down when he stares meaning in the face in the form of his new born child. I know the rest of the film is a complete Safdie chaos fest (good thing btw.) But when did we lose basic media literacy, this isn’t even a complex story. 

Gonna add in a list of reads and comments that I’m directly referencing here. 

The people who are adamant that the glorification of the ping pong match at the end undoes the point that you’re meant to dislike Marty, the whole point of the rocky style match is complete absurdity, his childs mother has been shot and is in surgery potentially gonna lose the baby (Due to his actions.) and the guy flew around the world to hit a ball with a paddle it’s absurd commentary, that’s pretty clear.

Any read, that views the film as a story of triumph that Marty achieved his dream so earned the right to settle down and become a father has completely missed the point. 

The idea that the story didn’t earn marty’s “change” or the idea that he “changed.” It’s not as much a change as it is an existential realisation when his own child is staring him in the face. It’s not more complicated than there’s nothing more meaningful than parenthood and something meaning more than the self, the director said as much himself.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There is an interview by the guy who made Evangelion that’s taking from the guy who made mobile suit Gundam that goes along the lines that at some point in the 1990s Japanese audiences didn’t want complex relationships in media anymore and I think that’s become true to many who watch things these days. I don’t think it’s everyone but enough that it’s a real trend that translates to some people getting frustrated with complex characters.
2. Both things can be true. I personally enjoyed it, but I can totally understand why people wouldn’t enjoy watching two and half hours of it. It doesn’t mean they have bad taste or don’t understand it - it just means they don’t like it. That’s the beauty of art, everyone has different perceptions of the same work.
  - One thing is to say that you didn't like it and another to say it's bad or unwatchable.

There's been a shift in online discourse the last few years that ignores overarching themes and story and focuses on characters and how relatable, likeable or morally righteous they are, likely stemming from fandom culture. I think that's a very shallow way to look at art and media.
    - Totally agree. Fandom culture but I’ve also wondered if it comes from the way screens are viewed now. Now that everyone is “on screen” so to speak (zoom, facetime, tiktok etc), everyone is performing, everyone is supposed to be likeable, and it’s getting harder for people who grew up in this world to differentiate between fiction and reality. Or at least between knowing when to park your morals to explore a character or situation.
      - Me arguing with my ex because I said I loved the character of Logan Roy and her not being able to hear anything other than “ I agree with what he stands for”.
    - I think this is the crux of the issue why people are missing the forrest for the trees with this film. They see the portrayal of Marty and his destructive pursuit of his “dream.” and assume portrayal equals endorsement and not absurdist commentary which it clearly is
    - Also people mistaking their personal opinions (“I didn’t like it”) with quality (“and therefore it was bad”).

One doesn’t necessarily mean the other.
3. Cool perspective, but wrong. Here's an excerpt from an interview with Josh Safdie:

"I believe the movie is a little bit about seeing your parents before you were born," Safdie said. "Like seeing pictures of them, and thinking, 'Huh, what was going on there?' What was their purpose? Well... I was their purpose.

"There's a lot of mystery in those photographs. You're very sculptable in youth.

And when you have a child, I think a part of you hardens. It just does. There's now one person, who for the rest of their life, will have a strict point of view on you," he added. "That's the new dream. The beauty of people who manage to remain forever young is that they can stay sculptable in the context of parenthood and not compromise."

The relationship between filmmaking and parenthood — and how both things can harden and soften a person simultaneously - is real, and as our conversation comes to an end, Safdie reverts to the vulnerability he talked about at the outset.

"I'm going to cry thinking about it," he said, when the subject of his own kids comes up. "It's cosmic."

  
Evidently does not share your same perspective
4. It always makes me laugh when someone starts a sentence with “everyone missing the point.” You’re not being pretentious. You’re being an ass. You can share your opinion without setting up as the denigration of a non-existent straw man.

The end is deliberately ambiguous and I haven’t bothered reading a ton of criticism, yet, but yes, there’s plenty of room for interpretation.
5. As someone who hated Marty but didn't hate the movie up until the last scene, this is the best I've heard it cogently described. But I still don't think it pulled it off.

As a side note, every point aimed at people who don't like the movie is a smug lecture about how you don't have to like characters for them to be interesting or a character doesn't have to get comeuppance for us to know they suck. But you can't just project all this onto a movie because it has the bones of antihero story (or whatever this is), it has to actually execute its goals successfully. There's always the option that the movie just didn't stick the landing.

>It’s not as much a change as it is an existential realisation when his own child is staring him in the face

I think this is a clear description of what the movie wants to achieve with the ending, the problem is I didn't buy it. I didn't buy it in Timmy's acting, I didn't buy it in the way the scene was shot.

Part of it, I think, is because of a lack of contrast. What you're describing is a paradigm shift in the character, where everything else about his life is contrasted to this moment, because it has "actual" meaning. But there's nothing arresting in the film's visual language, its design, its soundtrack that indicates this. A movie about Marty's chaotic, selfish, absurd life set to a relenting 80s score... doesn't hold up a mirror to that with just another 80s song (even if I guess the lyric "welcome to your life" sorta makes sense). The fact that there's no tonal change in the film's language just makes me think Marty's most likely faking it, just like everything else about him in the rest of the movie is fake.

I've made this comparison before but I think Kelly Reichart's *The Mastermind* is the perfect foil to this movie. Josh O'Connor's character in this movie is very similar to Marty, he's a hustler who leaves everyone who cares about him out in the cold because he thinks he's special enough to get away with it. In the final scene of the movie he gets arrested while trying to hide out in a protest because he stole an old lady's wallet, and the poignancy isn't in that he's finally getting comeuppance; it's the emptiness of the scene. Josh O'Connor is in basically every shot of the movie, then he's suddenly thrown into a van and driven off while the camera stays fixed on basically empty street (sans a few policemen chuckling and picking up the remains of the protest). This is the type of contrast I'm talking about to make an existential point about a character

## 2. zulawski

**Score:** 0
**Body:** i'm open to some weird shit but i swear people are lying about zulawski to be cool😭 i just don't get the love for his work... i've seen posession (hated it sorry) and somewhat enjoyed the third part of the night. tried to watch some others (on the silver globe, cosmos) and it's just too vague...like i love something that makes me think but give me a bit more. i can appreciate experimental stuff and there's some nice shots and all that, but I just don't see the vision 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I would recommend giving him one last try with 'The Devil' (1972)

Cosmos is like an extreme final statement on art house film, there's no use running your head into a wall repeatedly to see if you're missing out on something you simply may not enjoy.
2. I wonder what's the experimental stuff that you can appreciate. 

People aren't lying to be cool, there are plenty of coherent well thought out praises of his films explaining why they resonate with people. 

Anyways he explains a lot of what he's trying to do and where he's coming from​ in his audio commentaries. He's pretty erudite.
3. like i'm really trying to watch cosmos again right now ... there is a little something to it, like a car crash i can't look away from. art is subjective and it intrigues me - it's making me think and feel like "???" so maybe his vision is working, who knows
  - You’re not ready for Zulawski. I recommend Quentin Tarantino, David Fincher, Ryan Coogler, and then if you really want to venture way out there try Christopher Nolan but DO NOT watch Tenet because that will be too vague.

## 3. Inception is probably the most "perfect" film we have seen in the last 15 years

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I just rewatched this film and genuinely would like to hear an objection to this. Just summarizing my points quickly in this post this movie has:

* An incredible cast (probably Nolan's best)
* Great cinematography
* A high concept movie that is actually able to be processed by a focused watch
* 3-4 plotlines that are carried throughout the movie that all are interesting and resolve in satisfying way
* Deep emotional complexity in Cobb and Fishers personal relationships
* Outside of maybe 2 of Nolans movies this is maybe his most poignant underlying theme with the idea of escaping reality and what reality even is.
* Probably one of the best film scores ever.
* Some insane set pieces (particularly the hallway scene)
* Arguably the best action scenes Nolan has done outside of the batman movies
* Great ending that is open ended enough to where it is still being discussed today

Now Id love to hear peoples counterpoints but I will address my ideas on the common criticisms of the movie

The main criticisms I hear are that it is too exposition heavy which is probably the one I mainly agree with, but I feel for a movie that nearly requires it that it tends to make it feel natural and come up in the proper context rather than a random moment contrived to just dump exposition. Also there is just no way you make this movie under three hours without having moments of heavy exposition or concept explaining, its just too much to expect to understand for a general audience.

The other I hear is that the character development is underwhelming. This is somewhat true in my opinion but my biggest counter point to that would be that it is an action blockbuster at its core rather than a character study. That being said I think Cobb gets ample time to flesh out his core emotions and motivations in the movie and despite his shortcomings throughout the films you root for him to succeed and get back to his kids. The other characters get slight backstory through small bits of dialogue that I actually found quite refreshing, like knowing that Eames and Arthur have a history and a general rivalry with each other. Besides that I do think Fishers storyline is fleshed out well enough to tell us what we need to know about his character, even without knowing who he is as a person, you feel good for him getting to come to a catharsis about his father even if its built on a lie.

  
Id love to discuss the movie though as I have just rewatched it recently and would like to hear any other opinions or tidbits you have about the movie

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm agree with PlasticMercury and franjipane. However, I differ slightly in that I am generally not a fan of his work, although I like Inception more than his other films.

For me, his films rely too much on style and story gimmickry. His films often feel souless due to what I view is his stilted character writing. I think Inception "tricked" the audience into thinking that there was more character development due to the excessive expository dialogue. He also had the benefit of working with great actors who are able to do more with somewhat flat dialogue and formulaic scene construction.  
  
Nevertheless, when I try and gain something from this film, I can't get past the fact that it's simply a heist film with a love interest. I never feel what Leo's character is supposed to be feeling, even though he does an exceptional job at conveying this through his character. Instead, I am told and manipulated into what I should be thinking and feeling about his character, his motivations, and his desires. The father/son b-story is stereotypical to a fault and one single layer of brick in what should be layers upon layers.

In the end, Nolan is somewhat of a magician. He fools us with one hand by distracting us from the other. The soundtrack, which is wonderful, is used, as usual by Nolan, to excess. It's relentless and at times overbearing but it sure helps hammer home the emotional tone to the scene which was lacking in the script. The complex storyline he puts in front of the viewer is a tightrope between keeping the audience balanced and plummeting to a confused death. The viewer invests most of their active participation in deciphering details of the heist rather than an emotional connection to the characters.

Still, I take it for what it is. It was an amusement park ride geared for those above a certain height limit. It certainly doesn't have the complexity of Kubrick, or the philosophical focus of Bergman, or even the personal obsessions of Scorsese, nor should it. He's not that type of filmmaker and I'm OK with it.
  - Inception is not JUST a story about a love interest and a child. That’s very surface level, and no there’s no trickery.

There’s a dialogue from the film: “they’re dreaming: that’s their reality. Who are you to say it isn’t”.

And then of course there’s the ending, and Nolan and his wife have openly stated that the ending is not about whether Cobb is dreaming or not, it’s that Cobb doesn’t care. That’s the grander point inception is making. Cobb is someone who obsessed over reality, and by the end, the point is he doesn’t. He accepts living in a dream if it makes him happy. We often obsess over making people live in reality, and that’s often what a lot of people struggle with. If someone wants to be, let’s say, a musician, a common clap back might be “go back to reality, stop dreaming”. But a lot of time, reality sucks, and dreams are a way in which people cope with this. Who’s to say this is a bad thing to do?


It’s an interesting question, I feel. The thing with Nolan is his films are often packaged in a way to make it accessible so even if someone doesn’t fully get the subtext, they enjoy the movie simply for what it is on the surface, a fun heist movie. That actually is the best way to create meaningful work because meaning doesn’t take priority and has to be figured out by the audience.
2. Sure. It’s heavily plot-driven cinema. When the imperatives of narrative suffocate creativity and visual essence, what we’re left with is not great cinema. There’s a whole world out there, kid: open the door. You don’t even have to knock.
3. [deleted]
  - Im sorry if this comes off rude as its not my intention but this is one of the most faux intelligent self flattering post I've ever read. You are absolutely entitled to your opinion about what movie you prefer but to say that one is made for a certain audience that is less intellectual is so incorrect that I feel as though its projection. While the concept of the two movies is similar as they both involve dream machines that allow others to enter your dream, that is not enough to say that inception is drawn from this, nor is that a unique enough idea on its own to merit some sort of ownership of. Outside of the main concept the movies are entirely different and focus on completely different aspects of dreams and the ideas behind them. They are not made to be the same movie as one prefers to be an abstract character study that allows for a more emotional and surreal plotline while one is made to be a high intensity action heist movie that requires structure and exposition. They need to explain what's happening in the movie because there would not be a movie without it. You are comparing two forms of films that are entirely different and trying to hold them to the same standards and expectations.

---
